title: You Will Cycle Through a Million Billion Cells — and Remain You

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Two to three million of your cells die every second. Over a lifetime you cycle through roughly a million billion cells. At what point, if ever, does 'you' stop being you?

card.copy: You Will Cycle Through a Million Billion Cells — and Remain You

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two to three million of your cells die every second. Over a lifetime you cycle through roughly a million billion cells. At what point, if ever, does 'you' stop

beats.narration_text: Most cells in your body are replaced within seven years. Your gut lining: every 3 to 5 days. Your red blood cells: every 120 days. Your skin: every 2 to 3 weeks. You are substantially a different physical object than you were a decade ago.

production_viz.label: Gut: 5 days. Red blood: 120 days. Skin: 3 weeks. Most: 7 years.

beats.narration_text: If almost all of your material has been replaced, what persists? Is there something continuous from the you of 2015 to the you of today — and if so, what is it made of?

card.copy: Gut lining replaced every 5 days. Red blood cells every 120 days. Most cells replaced within 7 years. What — if anything — is continuous?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: If almost all of your material has been replaced, what persists? Is there something continuous from the you of 2015 to the you of today — and if so, what is it

beats.narration_text: Not all cells are replaced equally. Neurons in your cerebral cortex — the ones that store memories and define your personality — are mostly not replaced during your lifetime. They are born around birth and mostly live until you die. Your brain's wiring pattern is one of the most persistent parts of you.

production_viz.label: Cortical neurons: born ~birth, die with you. The most persistent cells.

beats.narration_text: The key insight is that identity is not in the atoms but in the pattern. I of t equals the configuration of relationships among cells at time t. The river is not the water molecules — it is the shape the water takes as it flows through. The molecules pass; the river persists.

production_viz.label: I(t) = configuration of relationships. River: molecules flow, shape persists.

beats.narration_text: The pattern can persist even as the substrate turns over, because cells are not replaced randomly — they are replaced according to templates. A new gut cell grows in the same position, with the same neighbors, as the one it replaced. The pattern is copied as the substrate refreshes.

production_viz.label: Replacement follows template: same position, same neighbors → pattern maintained

beats.narration_text: Henrietta Lacks died in 1951. Her cancer cells were cultured and have been reproducing ever since. Today there are at least 20 tons of her cells in labs around the world — more than 10,000 times her body weight alive in 1951. In what sense are these cells 'her'?

production_viz.label: HeLa: Henrietta Lacks 1951. 20 tons of cells in labs today. Are they 'her'?

beats.narration_text: The HeLa cells have her genome — the same DNA sequence. But they have none of her neural patterns, none of the cellular organization that constituted her. Continuity of genome is not continuity of self. The information pattern is more specific than the DNA.

production_viz.label: HeLa: same genome, no neural pattern. Genome ≠ self. Pattern > DNA.

beats.narration_text: The Ship of Theseus: every plank replaced, is it the same ship? Your answer: yes, if the replacements maintain the same structural relationships. The ship's identity is the pattern of connections between planks, not which specific wood. Your identity is the pattern of connections between neurons.

production_viz.label: Ship of Theseus: pattern of connections = identity. Same for neurons.

beats.narration_text: 2 to 3 million cells per second. Roughly 10 to the 15 cells over a lifetime. Gut: 5 days. Red blood: 120 days. Neurons: mostly lifetime. Identity = pattern I(t), not atoms. The information persists as the substrate flows through it.

card.copy: 10^15 cells over a lifetime. Atoms replaced; pattern persists. Neurons: lifetime. Identity = configuration I(t). You are the river's shape, not its water.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: 2 to 3 million cells per second. Roughly 10 to the 15 cells over a lifetime. Gut: 5 days. Red blood: 120 days. Neurons: mostly lifetime. Identity = pattern I(t)

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
